title: Normal flow and display

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Normal flow and display; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Normal flow and display
sources.claim: Normal flow lays out block and inline content in writing-mode order and provides the resilient baseline before specialized layout.

sources.claim: Normal flow lays out block and inline content in writing-mode order and provides the resilient baseline before specialized layout.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Normal flow lays out block and inline content in writing-mode order and provides the resilient baseline before specialized layout.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A document remains readable in source order before grid enhances the card arrangement. Your goal is to predict and repair layout using normal flow and appropriate display behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Box model, sizing and overflow; html semantics landmarks; Content, padding, border and margin; html document dom. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Normal flow lays out block and inline content in writing-mode order and provides the resilient baseline before specialized layout. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Floats, out-of-flow positioning, fragmentation, margin collapse, and replaced elements can alter expected flow. This draft therefore rejects: Modern layouts should remove every element from normal flow for precise control.

includes: Reason about block, inline and flow-root participation before choosing a layout system.
Predict and repair layout using normal flow and appropriate display behavior.
excludes: Floats, out-of-flow positioning, fragmentation, margin collapse, and replaced elements can alter expected flow.

outcomes: Predict and repair layout using normal flow and appropriate display behavior.

examples.title: Normal flow and display: concrete case
examples.situation: A document remains readable in source order before grid enhances the card arrangement.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A document remains readable in source order before grid enhances the card arrangement.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Preserve meaningful source and normal flow, then add layout without breaking reading order or content expansion.
examples.result: The result follows because Preserve meaningful source and normal flow, then add layout without breaking reading order or content expansion.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Modern layouts should remove every element from normal flow for precise control.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Preserve meaningful source and normal flow, then add layout without breaking reading order or content expansion.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Modern layouts should remove every element from normal flow for precise control.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Normal flow and display, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Normal flow and display before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
